steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank follows ‘three primary’ and introduces a list of three named periods. The left side — ‘Egyptologists have commonly divided ancient Egyptian history into three primary’ — is a complete independent clause. The right side is a list, not a new independent clause.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘periods. The’: a period ends the independent clause and begins a new sentence with ‘The Old Kingdom’. But ‘The’ then leaves the list floating without the noun it was promised to follow (‘three primary periods’). Creates a sentence fragment. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘periods; the’: a semicolon must separate two independent clauses. What follows (‘the Old Kingdom... and the New Kingdom’) is a list, not an independent clause with its own subject and verb. Misuse of semicolon. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘periods: the’: a colon after a complete independent clause correctly introduces a list or elaboration. The list of three kingdoms directly elaborates on ‘three primary periods.’ ✓
  • Choice D — ‘periods, the’: a comma alone is too weak to introduce a formal list after an independent clause — a colon is required here. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
